Marketing. There are lots of great advisors who will never get past a certain size. The best advisors, imho of course, are so finance-centric that the marketing aspect of sales eludes them.

We are not trained in: color matching, writing great ad copy, direct mail penetration rates, etc.

If we were rainmakers we'd go work for someone else and get paid a lot more money to make rain.

What we are, or, at least what we are supposed to be is well trained in investment management principles and techniques.

We should know taxes, estate planning, asset protection, and much more.

We should be on the way to obtaining or having, again imho, the following certifications:

Chartered Financial Analyst

Enrolled Agent

Chartered Market Technician

and

Chartered Investment Management Analyst

That brings me to problem number two.

Our biggest problem is ourselves. The vast majority of “stockbrokers" or “investment advisor reps" that I've seen over the years are trained sales people from other industries. Shoes, cars, gym memberships.

I dont know what the actual numbers are for people who hold those designations versus those who hold themselves out as advisors, but I suspect the number of well trained advisors is low.

The very best thing my industry could do is fire everyone and demand that only people who are well along the way to at least 2 of those certifications can hold themselves out as advisors. Moreover, they must be held on some type of probation until they receive all 4.

We manage money. Their lives are in our hands. We should bring the best we have to the task.

As per SEBI RIA (Registered Investment Advisors) regulations, which became effective from October 2020, the roles and responsibilities of a Distributor and a Advisor has been demarcated.

Mutual Fund Distributor (MFD) role has been confined to offering various financial products to their clients without offering any financial advice. They can't charge the client any fee for offerings Financial products, as their remuneration is inbuild or paid by the product manufacturers.

Earlier they were know as Independent Financial Advisor (IFA), now, they can't use it anymore and have to represent as MFD.

Whereas, Investment Advisor (IA) is expected to provide Financial Advice or guidance to their clients post due diligence i.e. understand client needs, wants, risk taking ability and willingness to take risks, Financial position, etc.

As per RIA regulations, Only IA are allowed to do Financial Planning.

IA can only charge Fee from the client's. They are required to offer Only Direct MF, PMS, etc.

They are NOT allowed to earn any revenue either directly or indirectly from the Product Manufacturers.

The objective of new SEBI guidelines is ensure clear demarcation between MFD and IA to enable client to have clear understanding on expectation as well as conflict of interests.

It is a noble intention. However, kindly note that just because of a IA Certification, does not necessarily means Only they work in the best of interest of clients. There are many MFD (earlier called IFAs) who have worked very hard in the best interest of their client's for many decades in creating and growing wealth and they still continue to do it.

Too little income

Before you can manage money, you have to have some, and it has to be enough to cover more than the basics. It's possible to economize a lot on food, clothing, transportation, and in some cases, housing. Despite what many well-off people believe, many poor people are actually very conscientious about economizing. But these costs are nonzero for most people, and there are limits to how much one can reduce expenses. (Parents of younger children, the military, and certain employers do supply them, and some people manage to make and grow most of their own needs at relatively little monetary cost.)

With too little income, there is constant stress, fear, and worry. If one thing slips, perhaps someone is sick or injured and misses work, whole lives can collapse.

It's well and good to address incautious spending, insufficient saving, financial literacy, tax optimization, and whatever else. If someone has not enough money to eat or to get a safe place to call home, the rest doesn't really matter.

Financial Advisors can change lives, but I often see some big mistakes that can drastically affect the results - even as bad as losing all investment gains made! With all the new-age scams out there, it really matters to know these mistakes - so pay attention!

Here’s the Most Common Mistakes:

Hiring a “Financial Advisor” who is not a Fiduciary

Anybody can be a “financial advisor”. It’s an unregulated term so the title means nothing. Not going to name names, but plenty of companies out there abuse this term. Just watch “The Wolf Of Wall Street” to see more on how that works!

On the other hand, there’s a key type of financial advisor called a “Fidicuary” and they are what you want. They are legally obligated to make financial decisions that are in your best interest. They are the good guys on your side, and they can even lose their license if they do anything deemed unethical by regulators.

You can find fiduciaries near you on websites like [ComparisonAdviser.com](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Advisors%20Keywords%20-%20Questions&utm_content=What%20are%20some%20mistakes%20that%20people%20can%20make%20when%20choosing%20a%20financial%20advisor%3F&qclid=1066508421222975761%7C211106241908221%7C0 "trk.heysaver.com") \- their network is exclusively vetted and regulated fiduciaries.

Hiring the first advisor you meet

Ideally you are looking for a long-term partner in this space. It’s best to interview more than one advisor before making your final decision. Do what feels right for you.

Not checking their credentials

This goes without saying…make sure the advisor has the credentials and licenses to handle your finances. It never hurts to ask about tests they have taken as well! As I mentioned above, the highest-standard are called [fiduciaries](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Ad

Too little income

Before you can manage money, you have to have some, and it has to be enough to cover more than the basics. It's possible to economize a lot on food, clothing, transportation, and in some cases, housing. Despite what many well-off people believe, many poor people are actually very conscientious about economizing. But these costs are nonzero for most people, and there are limits to how much one can reduce expenses. (Parents of younger children, the military, and certain employers do supply them, and some people manage to make and grow most of their own needs at relatively little monetary cost.)

With too little income, there is constant stress, fear, and worry. If one thing slips, perhaps someone is sick or injured and misses work, whole lives can collapse.

It's well and good to address incautious spending, insufficient saving, financial literacy, tax optimization, and whatever else. If someone has not enough money to eat or to get a safe place to call home, the rest doesn't really matter.

When my first husband left me with just a used car and I had to go to work to support myself and my two small girls! First, I had a breakdown after trying to be brave with my new job, coming home tired and with office dress and heels still on go to the store to try to get something to eat, or going to the cafeteria and sharing my kid’s meals. I was once queen of the campus at a university, best dressed, top ten woman, won trophies with shows of singing, dancing, choirs, and very popular, church-going good gal. This showed me now all is what it seems to be.

So, after the breakdown, I talked to doctors and they said “he” was the one who needed help. The very good psychiatrist said I had to remember “Shit Happens!” I was shocked at the language, but it changed me for good. I got well, got better at my job, endured almost death threats from my ex because I asked for money for my car battery. I finally made enough money to get me by, and met another man who seemed to be someone I could get along with and we married.

From then on, I saved money on my own, and when another breakup seemed to be emanate, because of alcohol, I actually did not take any money from this man but I had made thousands in real estate He tried AA , but he was addicted.

I took that money and invested it. I married a third time, and this was the charming, top salesman, who said he had changed from being a womanizer because I was the kind of woman he wanted all his life…well, I wanted to believe him.

He ran off with my heart and my money three years later. However, I knew how to get a divorce by this time, got a good lawyer and got the $300,000 he took from me back over the years. I invested wisely, and lived below my means.

You see, you have to be able to save even if you believe someone will take care of you. You can always share with them years later if they need anything, but please, depend on yourself and save for emergencies, and old age.

PS: I ended up with more money than the husband who stole from me even though he was a highly paid regional manager, he didn’t know how to handle money, he knew how to make it. God is good. Stay faithful to God, as He is the only one to trust…”In God We Trust,” is on our coins.

This is a great question. The simple answer would be, yes, however, I would venture to say that financial services are experiencing innovation rather than disruption. Though these innovations are seemingly disruptive to the players that have been in the industry for many years, there is a necessity to “get with the times” as the saying goes.

Companies who do not update their technical sophistication are not set up for optimized functionality. For instance, moving into the online environment is not exactly an innovation as it is a necessity. Most businesses have already made the leap into the online space. However, are some innovations we will see in financial services:

- Though APIs are a fairly new idea reaching various industries, they have been around for several years. Fixed income markets will be able to offer bonds via API. Treasury auction-like forums will crop up so that personal investors will be able to barter with large banks for bonds.
- Apple Pay will continue to farm out requests for credit to a number of lenders, leveling the playing field for the credit industry
- Traditional advisors will begin to partner with digital advisors in order to leverage knowledge and experience and optimize their clients’ portfolios.

This is a topic on which I have a very great interest. If you would like to continue our conversation further, please feel free to reach out to me.

This is not legal advice, I’m just laying out the basic issues.

First, any time you take anyone’s money, you have to abide by laws covering fraud and other issues. Any misrepresentation could lead to civil (getting sued by your investors) or criminal (the government asking for you to be put in prison) claims. So you’re well advised to have an experienced lawyer draw up documents and include only truthful information and legal disclosures for your protection. Then it’s important to do what your documents promise you’ll do.

Second, if you don’t want to register as an investment adviser (which is not hard to do) or follow rules for public sale of securities (which is much harder to do), you need to make sure you fall under exceptions laid out by the Securities and Exchange Commission. You can probably do that if you have only a few investors and you knew them before they invested. But if you solicit investments from the public or have many investors, it will be hard for you to avoid investment adviser and investment company regulations.

Third, even if you were investing only your own money, many hedge fund strategies have to be constructed carefully to comply market rules—such as rules against market manipulation and position limits.

Generally speaking, if you set up a fund then you’re paying the salary to yourself anyway, so it doesn’t matter what it is, except that you’ll probably pay more tax if you make it higher rather than lower.

The fund managers were entitled to a share of the profits they generated, assuming the fund had the income to pay that out. Small startup hedge funds don’t tend to be very profitable, if they’re profitable at all, so the point isn’t to get paid in the short term, it’s to establish a valuable business over the longer term.

At the entry level for bankers (the analyst training programs) the banks have a bunch of more-or-less equally qualified people who are trying to impress their bosses to get long-term positions. Some of them compete by quantity, not by quality, at least at the beginning, so there are a bunch who work crazy hours (and they do get paid good salaries, although not that great if you calculate it by the hour). Those who are particularly talented can find a way to impress their managers other than by how many hours they are willing to work.

At the higher levels, the very best do work hard, but those who can gain the confidence of clients and bring in huge deals for their banks can earn extraordinary amounts of money.

[How can I start a hedge fund after college?](https://www.quora.com/How-can-I-start-a-hedge-fund-after-college/answer/Marco-Venti "www.quora.com")

01. Pass Series 63 exam
02. Pass Series 65 exam
03. Register as an investment avidser.
04. Register an LLC with your state's Secretary of State. The LLC will be an "investment avisor firm" and will be the General Partner of the hedge fund, which will be a Limited Partnership.
05. Register the Limited Partnership with your state's Secretary of State. Your Hedge fund.
06. Set up a bank account for the LLC. order checks
07. Set up a bank account for the LP, order checks
08. Get a Federal Employment Identification Number for LLC from [Internal Revenue Service](http://irs.gov/ "irs.gov")
09. Get a FEIN for the LP from [Internal Revenue Service](http://irs.gov/ "irs.gov")
10. File form ADV: Register the LLC as the Register Investment Advisor by filing Form ADV with [http://iard.com](http://iard.com/ "iard.com")
11. File form U-4: Register yourself - you personally, not either of the companies - as an investment adviser rapresentative of the Investment Adviser Firm (the LLC)
12. Create the following for the Investment Advisory Firm (i.e. the LLC): (a) by Laws, (b) compliance manual, (c) code of ethics, (d) supervisory procedures manual, (e) Investment Adviser/ Portfolio Managment Agreement, (f) Website, business cards, stationery, software, etc
13. and for the Hedge fund (i.e. the LP): (a) A limited partnership agreement that all partners wil have to sign, (b) private placement Memorandum, (c) a presentation or brochure about the proposed investment style (i.e. the sales pitch), (d) a custodian/brokerage account to house the fund's securities, (e) a prospectus
14. Register the LP interests offering with the SEC under rule 506 of regulation D of the Securities Act of 1933. Get a CIK number form the SEC and file Form D with the SEC and with each state in which you plan to offer or sell interests.
15. Issue a press release for the new hedge fund

If you pass the tests at the first try, after filing you paid $766.

I started my business in 1991 at the age of 23.

At the time, and for a few years after, I was almost nearly broke and barely earning a sustainable living on a meager income from the profits that my business was making.

My business was somewhat profitable, but, I knew that my only hope of growing a larger, and more profitable and thriving business, and building personal wealth, was to focus on business growth, and in order to do that, I had to minimize every dollar I extracted from the business, and focus on five core things:

- Take significant but calculated business risk while mitigating the downside where possible
- Maximize business profits and use those profits to build a larger more sustainable business
- Build the business into as much of a recurring revenue stream as possible
- Reinvest a percentage of the business’s profits outside of the business
- Married the most awesome wife who supported my business ventures and helped raise our family

I was able to do the above for 27 years and sold my business for 8-figures in 2017. In the meantime, I invested a large percentage of the business’s profits in buying apartment buildings.

I’m now 51 and retired. Retired, not in the traditional sense of sitting on the beach, but, my wife and I are now able to travel, and I can work on things that I’m excited about. We can more than live off the interest and dividend income and profits coming from other sources, and let the balance of my wealth compound.

My next project will be to start a sustainable foundation so that I can help the world, and know that I, and my family, have made a difference.

The picture below is of me in Dubrovnik this past summer.

The short answer is you can’t. The legal work alone for an actual hedge fund will be considerably more than 50K. And since ‘hedge funds’ are restricted to accredited investors, you need to be talking about considerably more size before what you’re building can be called ‘a hedge fund’. You’ll also need some size to justify the cost of compliance with the dizzying multitude of regulations.

You can form an ‘investor club’, or some trading entity at a small size, but not a hedge fund. And since about 50% of the problems that Hedge Funds solve in order to produce high enough returns to justify a 2 and 20 fee schedule have to do with managing liquidity, you won’t be able to scale that into an ‘actual’ hedge fund, nor learn how to solve the liquidity problems that hedge funds deal with every single day.

When last I checked, Prime Brokers treated 50 Million as the minimum level for dealing with a ‘hedge fund’, and even then, only when it was run by someone who has a history, or was otherwise is well known in the business. It may well be higher now. That’s usually the cutoff.

Below that there are still things you can do to trade. But whatever they are, they aren’t ‘hedge funds’.

As a lifelong trader and investor-

Hedge funds are limited to the wealthy because of their riskiness, high cost, and financial complexity.

Honestly, having analyzed the offerings of literally hundreds of hedge funds, and invested in some, I regret eating my time, energy and money on them. I am not big on government regulation but I’m very glad that people are protected from them. My advice to my family, friends, and Quora is to not bother with the entire class in general.

It’s very time consuming to analyze them. My track record for picking them really was good, but yet they failed to outperform the S&P 500 after expenses.

The rejected ones did even worse. And after spending much time going through them, picking about one in twenty five, I assure you, you’re not missing a thing.

Professional traders use no load broad based mutual funds or and ETF to invest. Hope this is helpful.

I would recommend that you just dump your money into Wealthfront and forget having a private wealth manager. Chances are, Wealthfront will do far better than any human, and will not have the agency conflicts that human wealth managers have.

If you choose to ignore my advice, by far the most important question that you need to ask is:

Are you a fidiuciary?

This is a legal term and if the person does not answer yes, or doesn't know, then he's a crook.

The next most important question is:

Who is your custodian?

A custodian is a 3rd party that actually holds the assets in your name. The custodians generates the statements (financial as well as tax statements), and if you fire your wealth manager, the custodian ensures you still have your assets and can sell and cash out as needed. The big red flag that famous cases such as Bernie Madoff's had was that the wealth manager did not use a well known 3rd party custodian (such as Schwab, Fidelity, etc) and generated his own statements. This let him cook the books and give you any number he liked, and we all know how that turned out.

I wrote an entire book because there are issues of technical competence involved when hiring a wealth manager (most are sales people rather than people who know their stuff), but those are the two most important questions that come to mind.

Interesting question. First off if you have come into enormouse wealth congratulations. There are a few things you can do to help you manage this wealth.

1. Build your Team of Professionals who are legit and trustworthy. For eg. accountant, wealth fund manager (be careful here and ensure the fees are not excessive), solicitor etc.
2. Don’t touch over 3/4s of the $$$ for at least 3 - 6 months to help you get over the headiness of the $$$. It’s easy to go out and spend cash willy nilly, however now is not the time to do so.
3. Seek to pay out any debts you owe, so you are debt free and ensure you remain debt free. In other words don’t immediately go back into debt or start spending wildly on the credit card/s. I personally recommend ditching the credit card/s altogether.
4. If you don’t own your own home, do the research only at this stage on a home you would like to live in. Nothing flashy or mansion type (even if you do have the $$$ for this). A home that has good bones and you’re happy to live in either by yourself or with your family. Avoid simply going out and buying the first home you see. Be careful. Do your research and due diligence first, second and third before parting with $1 of your money.
5. If you’re happy in your current home, do the research to do all those minor tasks that you have been putting off. Again research and due diligence is key here. Again you don’t want to go out and spend money willy nilly. If you’re happy with what you have all the better.
6. With the help of your accountant, set up a system where you pay yourself, fortnightly or monthly and then set yourself a budget on how to utilise this money. For example: If you’re happy to live on $2K per fortnight, then your budget and all relevant living expenses is nutted out on this $2K. Get my drift. Again now is not the time to go silly. The aim is to be careful however give you some wiggle room.

Well they’re my top 6 suggestions. Once again congratulations. Be Careful. Be Sensible and most of all Be Wise.

To Your Continued Success.

We'd implement 15-20% increases across entire ZIP codes, knowing regulators would take months to review. By then, we'd collected millions in extra premiums.

Claim Frequency Was Irrelevant Your rates weren't really based on how often you'd claim - they were based on how likely you were to shop around. A customer with 3 claims who got quotes every year paid less than a claim-free customer who never compared rates. It was pure price discrimination.

We'd implement 15-20% increases across entire ZIP codes, knowing regulators would take months to review. By then, we'd collected millions in extra premiums.

Claim Frequency Was Irrelevant Your rates weren't really based on how often you'd claim - they were based on how likely you were to shop around. A customer with 3 claims who got quotes every year paid less than a claim-free customer who never compared rates. It was pure price discrimination.

We Loved Policy Confusion Complex policy language wasn't an accident. The more confusing your coverage, the less likely you'd comparison shop effectively. We'd change terminology between companies deliberately to make apple-to-apple comparisons nearly impossible.

When I have a portfolio, at any given time in a bear/bull market, I trim down positions which are quite overvalued (but at a profit). Typical low net income, low profit margin, high price/earnings stocks with little capital/buffer and cyclical revenues. So I never keep those positions for very long, regardless the state of the market (bear/bull). I do this, because these stocks drop the most (fat gets trimmed), and often do so before the downturn officially begins.

In general, because I do keep some positions in a downturn, I hedge quite a bit. Either through a bit of day trading with mini futures or through options.

Any particular loss out of a stupid mistake can be rectified during the conference calls in the midst of a downturn. Investors generally behave much more anxious when “everything goes to shit”, so the fluctuation/volatility before and after publication of results is easy to profit from. With extreme leveraged positions, fine-tuned in an algorithm so you still get a few percent extra given you get a better bid/ask than average Honkey Joe at at home.

So in general

1. I never sell everything during a downturn.
2. I always sell or trim down extremely over valued stocks.
3. I take advantage of fear during a downturn through the use of basic algorithms.
4. I don't care about extra diversification due to a (upcoming) downturn. I invest what I believe in, I don't need to buy extra exposure to diversify my portfolio. Sometimes I am overexposed in a particular industry, but that will always be for good reason. Only portfolio managers in a bank need this kind of diversification given they need to pitch/sell their strategy to clients.

First, is it your job to ensure your work remains complaint? Is that your position in the company, to try and meet all state and local laws? If it is, I’m sure there are state and local websites that provide information on whatever regulations you need to meet.

If it is not your job, your actual position in the company to keep the company compliant, then I wouldn’t get involved in it.

There have been two times where I thought that my company was engaged in questionable actions.

In both cases I simply looked for other employment. One time it was a very large corporation, and I sent an email detailing my concerns to HR after leaving the company. The other was a small business, and there was no point to sending anyone an email because it’s likely they either knew, or they would not have cared either way.

Unless you are an executive somewhere, where you think your long term career and reputation will be called into question, and thus you need to document what is happening to protect yourself… there is really no benefit to you being a whistle blower.

Honestly, there just isn’t. At worst you’ll be labeled a trouble maker, and future employers won’t want you. At best you be ignored, or sidelined, where you never get promoted again.

No one is going to pay you a higher salary or defend you to the company. You are just making problems for yourself, with zero benefit.

The only way I would actually get involved is if they are selling weapons to the Chinese, or putting cyanide in baby food or something. If they are doing that, fine.

But if huge crime is that they don’t have the labor laws posted in the break room, and that’s your big failure to be compliant or something…. I wouldn’t get involved. It’s not worth it. You will just harm your own future career, and largely achieve nothing.

Many years ago, I ran into a guy that was making $10/hour at a crappy entry level job. He went on to explain that he had filed a lawsuit against a company, very big company here in Ohio, and he won. He proved the company violated the law.

Well the court issued their ruling, money was put into a pot, and then the company filed claim after claim, motion after motion, and by the time it was all said and done, the employees ended up with a check for a few hundred dollars each. But since this guy was the ring leader that pushed the lawsuit, the company isolated him, gave him the absolute worst jobs in the company, and all his promotions were gone and possible pay increase were like 3 cents a year.

But to make matters worse, he couldn’t get another job. Every employer did checks and called around, and discovered that he was involved in suing his employer, and no one would hire him. So he ended up making $10/hour at a crappy entry level job, even though he had years and years of experience.

So I always tell people, unless you actually consider all your options and rationally and intelligently determine this is a hill you are willing to die on, just put in your two week

You have hit upon a crucial question. Many marriages break up due to financial disagreements. My wife and I decided early on that we would not be one of those couples. It’s very likely our system would not work for others, and after talking to many of my friends about it, it seems there are as many different schemes for finances as there are marriages.

The first rule, the only rule that I think is unbreakable, is you need to devise a system that has buy-in from both of you. If one partner dictates how money will be earned, saved, and spent, you’re asking for trouble. Resentments will inevitably grow, and could easily put your marriage at risk. Don’t do that. Have one or a series of frank conversations, and negotiate a system that you are both into.

Unlike many couples I’ve spoken to, we handle our finances more separately than joined. I read once in a Suze Orman book (before she got kooky) that it’s important to acknowledge that marriages do form a new unit, a union of two people, but that doesn’t mean there aren’t still two individuals. Both people have their own identities, ideas, and dreams. I personally believe that totally subsuming that for the good of the union, and thus only having joint accounts, is a mistake.

This also keeps in mind an uncomfortable truth- many marriages won’t make it. By having separate accounts, it keeps both people involved in finances and budgeting. Many marriages have only one person handling financial matters, and if the marriage dissolves, the other person is terribly equipped to go forward.

So this is what we do- we have a main checking account for our joint expenses. This includes mortgages and all joint bills like power, gas, garbage, mobile, and TV. Given our individual incomes, we each contribute the same percentage of income to the joint account. If I make more, I contribute more. This isn’t a hard-and-fast rule for us, but it provides a basic level of fairness.

All other income is held in individual accounts. I believe very strongly that everyone should have some amount of money that they can spend without regret or having to answer to anyone. My wife and I have different ideas about money. I tend to be a little more frugal, but she is still very conscientious. The fact that she can go out and spend money on something I consider trivial is important to me, and it’s important that I don’t see it, because it’s not my money. If it came out of a joint account, I might feel like a money cop, and get upset. It doesn’t, and I don’t.

Another part of this (which should happen regardless of your system) is you have to be completely and totally open about everything. My wife technically doesn’t have the login and password to my accounts, but I will happily log in and tell her to the penny how much I have. She’s entitled to that information whenever she wants. Oh boy, where do I even start? After 8 years as an auto insurance agent, I have zero loyalty left to protect these companies.

We Had "Loyalty Lists" Every month, I'd get a report of customers who hadn't shopped around in 2+ years. These were our golden geese - we could raise their rates aggressively because they'd proven they wouldn't leave. One customer I remember was paying $3,200 annually for coverage that should have cost $1,800. She stayed for 5 years.

The "File and Use" Scam Here's something most people don't know: in many states, insurance companies can raise your rates immediately and justify it later. We'd implement 15-20% increases across entire ZIP codes, knowing regulators would take months to review. By then, we'd collected millions in extra premiums.

Claim Frequency Was Irrelevant Your rates weren't really based on how often you'd claim - they were based on how likely you were to shop around. A customer with 3 claims who got quotes every year paid less than a claim-free custome

During my twenty year journey from founder and CEO of a bootstrapped startup the main times that challenges kept me up at night were as follows :

During the first five years until we posted our first profit funding and management of resources and cash was the one constant factor that kept me up at night as the “establishment” and hence investors were convinced our business model wouldn’t get the critical mass to be successful

Three years into our startup our US business partners filed for bankruptcy which forced us to pivot our business model and to divert time and resources to a legal process to avoid us getting dragged into legal disputes

When we finally got to profitability the pressure valve was released and although we still had barriers to overcome on a daily basis I could sleep fine at night

The next time I lost sleep worrying about things at work was in the year prior to exit when I was working 24/7 keeping the business steady whilst also working with an investment bank, lawyers and accountants to manage a sale process which took a full year before we closed our ultimate exit at $215 million

Bunt out at the end of the sale process I was ready for my early retirement six months later as the purchaser could see that we had the C-level team in place to run the day to day business without the need for my input

The patience to stay invested for the long term or have a long term view on one’s investment.

It is important to have the right asset allocation and also invest in quality schemes which generate superior risk adjusted returns.

But the key aspect to benefit from the power of compounding is to stay invested for the long term in asset classes like equity.

We see investors wanting to redeem their investments when those have made decent profits, thinking that they will lose this growth if the markets fall.

Or even trying to stop investing or redeem when they do not see the expected growth within some time.

The opportunity loss here is much higher than one can perceive.

Lets take an example-

You start investing Rs 10,000 in equity via the SIP route every month for your kids education goal which is 20 years away. Lets assume it earns a long term avg return of 12 % pa/yearly.

But you stop and redeem it after 5 years - You get Rs 8.11 Lakh

If you redeem it after 10 years - You get Rs 22.4 Lakh

If you hold on for 15 years - You get Rs 47.6 Lakh

And if you manage to hold on for 20 years ,till you need to use it for the goal, what is the final corpus?

Rs 1 Crore !

As the time period of the investment increases, the growth is exponential.

Strangely, people tend to keep fixed income investments like FDs for long term, where the multiplying factor is low due to lower growth rate, but not the assets which are actually meant to be invested for the long term.

It depends on the business itself and its needs. There are various options:

1. Hire a full-time CFO: undertakes all the analysis and modelling required by the business to ensure appropriate budgeting / planning and support the business’ strategy, in particular growth plans, profitability (maximisation and margins)
2. Part-time CFO: For smaller businesses who don’t need or can’t afford a full-time CFO. We can get expensive.
3. Financial Consultant: Similar to part-time CFO, but you get a bit more flexibility with how the contractual obligations are agreed with them - e.g. can have project-specific fees or hourly charge-out rates. Can call themselves any number of things (Finance Business Partner, Advisor, etc) so look for the right skill set and experience.
4. Use an online tool: Still requires time investment by the business and some level of expertise. But there are various tools available that can reduce the total end cost.
5. Combination of 3 and 4: probably your best bet. Just note - some consultants will try to under-utilise or underplay the importance of 4 above as that means their invoice amount is higher.

This happened to me.

When a research student in theoretical physics in my 20s, I used to live in a cubby hole, and eat 1 warm meal a week, for which I walked about 10 miles. For food, the rest of the week, I lived on bread, butter, milk, cereal, nectarines and yoghurt. To feel physically free and alive, I ran hard in the park, lungs joyously gasping for breath. And to feel mentally and spiritually alive, I spent most of the rest of my time in thinking - reading or reflecting to marvel at the gift of life betowed upon me to ponder this universe.

My body and stomach fed, my mind fed - and my heart alive and fed.

So I was rich. Or to be exact, rich in time and experience. Every minute of existence, every day.

Of course, I struggled for money - to put it mildly. But I also saw people who had money but no time - spending all their days in their jobs. They seemed to struggle to find time.

And even when they found some time, they seemed to waste that precious time and hard earned money on clothes and restaurants instead.

I therefore didn't see them as rich. Sure they could get things I couldn't, but then I had something they didn't. It was just a matter of choice.

A thought then occured to me. What if a person could have both money and time? Ideal right? But even then something told me this likely led to other issues - in the absence of some necessary scaffolding in character. But actually, it's worse.

Money can make the scaffolding collapse.

I thought then if you had both money and time, you likely had no meaning left in life. Or at least the risk of losing meaning.

30+ years later I have realised that all 3 states of life are not just possible and true, but are a common state of affairs for many.

And there is a 4th state, which luckily I never experienced. That of having no money, and no time. The is the state for billions in the developing world. They work every waking hour to source food and shelter for their loved ones. These are the unsung heroes of humanity.

See below for example.
[https://fb.watch/mLfe9CvH8t/](https://fb.watch/mLfe9CvH8t/ "fb.watch")
Now back on topic.

Trying to hold on to meaning when you have both money and time is hard for most people. Trust me. Most people don't adapt to this situation well. And I have had my challenges too.

You see you can be a billionaire flying around the world seeing everything there is to be seen. Or dream about it.

And what if I had ever become a billionaire?

But could that billionaire have ever matched the experience I had as a simple student of quantum field theory? That long walk for a warm meal on Sunday evenings?

Think of the sequence: a 10k run in 42 minutes, followed by a cold shower, then 5 mile walk to a Chinese restaurant for a £2.40 plate of chicken and black bean sauce and unlimited jasmine tea? A 5 miles walk on empty stomach, followed by 5 miles walk home on full stomach, and the only warm meal of the week.

What was the experience like? Ki

I don’t want to write yet another answer about how the big problem is that everyone else is dumb and doesn’t do everything the way I do. “The big problem is that my project manager is stupid and people don’t write enough documentation.” That’s not a “big problem.” That’s just an everyday gripe we all have with our job and it will probably never go away. Instead of that, I want to focus on something that I believe to be a real, systemic issue that can possibly be fixed.

The big problem in software engineering is this: it is treated as an academic pursuit rather than a trade. As a result, there is currently no straightforward way to train new engineers.

Let me give you an analogy. Think of coding like golf. The junior engineer can use the driver and get the ball all the way to the green, but might need help with a senior engineer to get the intricacies right, because the hardest part is putting, that last little bit that requires a depth of knowledge of detail.

The problem is this: how, exactly, do you take someone who is an absolute beginner and get them to the junior engineer stage? How do you take someone who can’t even handle a club right and teach them to drive? Taking a junior engineer up to senior is less of an issue. That just takes time, patience, and guidance, because the foundation is already there. The hard part is laying the foundation to begin with; taking a total newbie and getting them to the point where they can do work as a junior. This is why the one to three years of a person’s engineering career are the most hectic. It’s one big scramble, a chaotic time when you’re drinking from a firehose and learning a hundred things at once.

Once a person has become a “junior,” it mellows out a bit. It’s not so hard to identify weaknesses and improve them once a structure is already in place. The hard part is taking someone who has no structure whatsoever and trying to get them to the stage where they’re recognizably some kind of developer. That first step is the hardest, if you ask me.

Getting a CS degree doesn’t teach you to work as an engineer. I’ve worked with fresh graduates and with fourth-year undegrads, as a teacher and as a coworker on actual jobs. They seldom know what the hell they’re doing. This goes back to what I said earlier: when you treat this as an academic pursuit rather than a trade, you make more difficult than it needs to be.

I think that the industry would benefit immensely from the widespread use of an apprenticeship model. Of course, this is easier said than done, because companies want to turn a profit and nobody wants to be the one to train the newbies. Trying to get rich quickly.

It takes time, there are no shortcuts. You need to outwork your competitors AND work smarter than them.

I’m working 24/7, so dealing with life events and other things are a challenge. Then again, these things are fairly easy to deal with once the money starts coming and there are proper systems for scaling set in place. Normally, people who work a lot have time to deal with things like this in the weekend, but that is hard when you hardly ever have a proper weekend.

Staying focused can be a challenge too, but is a requirement to really get anywhere. You need to completely rid yourself of “shiny object syndrome”, and focus on one thing until it works.

Dealing with your social life is also a challenge. I only have time to really sustain one conversation in addition to my business partner. I wish I could pause my entire social life for a full year to get to where I want to be. The good thing is that your family and real friends will understand your situation, and won’t leave you. They will also benefit from your success anyways.

Conclusion: I recommend ditching the “get rich quick” mentality, and rather focus on how you can improve yourself and your performance to learn to execute at a high level, while also being prepared to give up a lot of comfortable things on the way. Getting rich quick is an illusion. Improve your health, stop eating junk and sugar, ditch the soda, start meditating, and figure out why you want to get rich, and why it’s important to you.

Getting rich is a side-effect of being passionate about something meaningful you spend all your waking time on. I now have money, but lack time. And I still keep on working to get to where I want. At least I can afford getting a cleaner and a better diet to keep me going, and hopefully free up a little time on weekly routines.

I constantly find myself playing with tech tools.

It’s normal for me to stay up late at night because I’m too engrossed optimizing my business’ tech stack…

Very problematic.

The same applies to my CRM.

I asked and browsed way too many questions on Quora. I bugged way too many forums about the right CRM and email tools.

I researched way too much about how different apps can integrate with each other.

* * *

At the end of the day, I realized that it’s not about the tool.

It’s just not about the tool.

It’s just not about the tool.

Here’s the thing: if there are 5 good tools for the same job, does it matter which one you choose?

What matters is the process.

What matters is you take a path and make everything work nicely together.

What matters is you execute rather than whiteboard your tech stack imaginations.

* * *

I highly recommend focusing on what a CRM means to you.

Sales, customer happiness, loyalty, and referrals.

That’s the kind of stuff you should focus on.

Focus on identifying the customer journey. Come up with a funnel.

See the common patterns of leads you close. Understand what it takes to get a lead on the phone.

The problem is getting distracted with the idea of having a CRM… versus what the CRM actually means to the business.

I invite you to:

- Understand your pipeline
- Note the most important customer information that you’ll record
- Maintain a low-cost CRM by using a spreadsheet or some other simple tool

Once your process is super clear, you’ll be in the right position to choose the right tool and to set it up.

Thank you for the A2A.

Most of the challenges are generally related to the estimating technique, but there is a common denominator, which is the idea, broadly accepted today, that we are quite bad at estimating time.

We could blame the Protestant Work Ethic for pushing us into maximising utilisation and putting employees under eccessive pressure, or we could simply accept that time estimates are conditioned by specific, personal circumstantial conditions and for their very same nature they are unreliable.

This can be easily demonstrated if we ask a heterogeneous group of people, as it might be a project team, to estimate the time it would take to run their way to office using the stairs: fitter, healthier and optimistic individuals will keep their estimate much lower than their unfit, pessimistic colleagues.

To wrap up:

1. When estimating using abstractions such as Story Points, the biggest challenge is perhaps convincing your team to completely divorce "complexity", or whichever abstraction you are using, from time.
2. When estimating with time, the challenge is finding a way to "embrace" the natural uncertainty embedded in time estimates.

Note, in case you are wondering, the solution to 1) is normally a proper training for your team (do not ask how long they think it takes to run the stairs, ask instead how many stairs they think they must run); the solution to 2) is in range estimating techniques.

As you may be aware, the work portfolio of a CFO is extremely large and demanding. Some portions of it are to do with discipline and relentless follow ups when it comes to areas like controls, compliances, etc…which can be significantly eased by setting up of a framework, metrics and dashboards, using which you can track and make sure that those areas are working smoothly. Let me address your question by addressing the much more challenging areas. A CFO is expected to have a rounded perspective of the business and hence he is trusted by the CEO to participate in all key strategy matters requiring decision making and it is here that the CFO needs to work with a lot of ambiguity and use his intellect, skills, experience and gut to help the CEO make the right decisions. No clear data will be available in many situations, rather it will be unclear and confusing at times. It is here that experience comes into play. With a solid experience of over 20 years, these parts can be handled with significant ease. Helping the CFO devise the strategy, assess and manage risks, raise funds, controlled delegation, reporting and analytics on a timely basis providing actionable insights, contracts, commercials and large vendor negotiations are all challenging aspects of the role.

The lesser challenging parts are the ones to do with setting up processes, internal controls, measuring and monitoring cash flows, improving profitability, tax planning, outsourcing non core activies and managing the banking relationship. With a good accountant which could be a CA or a cost accountant or any other, these parts can be attained as a team. These are also the parts where the CFO can coach and mentor the accountants and bring them up the value chain, enabling them take more senior roles down the line.

The more challenging parts dwelt earlier consume most of the time of a CFO. Experience helps here.

While your question is pretty complete, I would definitely add: IS your IDEA or VISION for the business “good enough” that it will gain you customers and revenue? Many businesses get started on a bad idea, and by the time they find out it doesn’t work as well as they thought.. it can be too late. Be sure that the idea you are planning to build your business on will really work! The next obvious thing is Priority: what when, where and how much TIME something will need from you? Your family is important.. but you just got a call from a big customer who needs you right NOW to accomplish something for them.. who wins the top priority? And that leads me to the biggest concern of all: prioritize your tasks and plans against your day-to-day needs.. who wins and who loses has to be decided! If you are running around dealing with the “urgent” items all the time, you are having a fire drill.. and that doesn’t always put out - or prevent - the biggest fires quickly enough!

Never is there enough money in a startup environment, so, you have to “do without” sometimes.. for example, you pay all your workers and vendors.. and not end up with enough to pay yourself.. and this is common for many small, underfunded, and understaffed enterprises. The burnout comes when you are overextended for too long.. SO, one remedy is to take time! Find some low cost and time “incentives” to reward yourself when you have a win.. Lack of support? Can be tough.. when you must rely on businesses that charge you for services you can’t initiate yourself.. but the biggest lack of support has to be that your family isn’t supporting what you’re doing.. the worst possible failure of all!

All of these have rapped my knuckles at least a bit, but for me, the BIG ONE is: if someone doesn’t want what I want to sell them, it’s going to be a losing fight all the way (the bad idea issue). Also, businesses can charge pretty exorbitant rates for seemingly “simple” things (this was especially true regarding Internet and online Marketing for my enterprises). Moreover, if that vendor’s schedules are completely full.. this will force you to work with a less than desirable vendor (and often the “handholding” time commitment with it).. or, you have to suck it up, buttercup, and pay up!

To be fair here, I have had startup businesses, and have enjoyed myself immensely over decades as my own boss or executive (even when it got tough). It isn’t for everyone, not all who think they want to do this have the business acumen.. they just like that they can be their own boss.. which sometimes, carries a worse “ouch” than getting chewed out by your boss! I was talking to a Venture Capitalist early on about our company. Out of the blue the VC looked at me and said, “Well you’re the CEO, so you get to deal with investors like me.

“And that’s why you get more stock than the others.”

The VC was playing to the myth that your biggest pain and problems as CEO are dealing with investors. In one sense the VC wasn’t wrong, but he wasn’t right either.

Sure, dealing with investors can be a real painful experience. I should know because I certainly felt that pain all too well.

However, I never felt that dealing with investors was something I didn’t want to do. In fact, my thought process was pretty simple.

I want my destiny in my own hands, not anyone else’s. So I am happy to meet with investors and deal with investors and fight with investors.

Why would I want to self-inflict the pain of dealing with investors on myself?

The easy answer is because I am in control of my destiny and the company’s destiny. I wouldn’t want anyone else but me handling that.

Even though I had my share of difficulties dealing with our investors, I never felt it was something I didn’t want to do. And, quite frankly, dealing with investors didn’t really take a huge amount of time unless we were in fund-raising mode.

And, even in fund-raising mode, I recognized that it was part of the job. So I always went at raising money in a full-force way.

I networked. I pitched. I put everything I had into our efforts to raise money. And, crazy me, I actually enjoyed raising money!

Not all of it. I certainly didn’t like hearing investors tell us no.

But I really liked raising money.

There’s an adrenaline rush you get when you pitch and you connect with your audience.

Here are the other 10 things that are the biggest pains about being a startup CEO:

A. You can never take a second off. You always have to be on.

I’m not saying you can’t take a vacation, but I am saying that you can never, ever have a down moment as a CEO.

Your team is watching you from the minute you walk into your office until you go to bed at night. Literally everything you do and everything you say is being analyzed and overanalyzed.

Nothing is analyzed more then when you have to let someone go. That leads to…

B. You have to fire people, and firing people sucks.

You’re going to make hiring mistakes. And some of your cofounders are not going to work out.

You owe it to your team, your investors, and yourself to fire the people that don’t work out. You will lose the really good members of your team if you don’t fire the people that don’t work out.

That doesn’t make firing people easy. And that doesn’t make firing people so

Unless you have a ton of management experience, I would advise caution about accepting this responsibility. You say that your fiancée's parents are thinking of "retiring soon". Unless "soon" means in a couple of years or more, you would be stepping into a very difficult situation.

They have a successful business and are thinking of handing it over to their new son-in-law. This is a huge mistake. If they aren't willing to stay on for a few years so that you have time to learn the business and for customers and employees to get to know you and become comfortable with you, the likelihood of failure is high. How would your new wife feel about you if you trashed her parents' successful business.

I would make it clear to the parents that you are willing to work with them to ensure a smooth transition, but only if they are willing to spend the time and energy to help you get up to speed. This isn't likely something that can happen in a few months.

It’s a bit like marriage.

There’s the initial honeymoon period where everything is in front of you, you’re writing up your business plan, finding premises, buying equipment and dreaming of an amazing future. You throw yourself enthusiastically into your new business and spend every hour of the day on it, loving every minute of it. You get your first customer, the buzz is amazing. You get your first payment, you feel liberated. Things can only get better from here.

12 months in, the initial growth spurt has tapered off, the buzz has worn down and you’re facing your first cashflow crisis. A customer hasn’t paid, a supplier has let you down, your shop has been vandalised… at least one curveball has been thrown your way to destabilise your dream. You have also realised by now how much of a grind it is to keep things going. You have to wear so many hats: accountant, salesperson, customer service, recruitment, marketing, fulfilment, etc. You start making sacrifices and cutting corners to keep things manageable; dirty solutions that weren’t part of your perfect plan a year ago. You have to take out another credit card to pay a supplier. You have to borrow money from your family to pay your staff. You don’t have enough time to focus on your product or service, and everything feels rushed and half-assed. You get some negative comments from customers. It’s very disheartening.

You contemplate divorce. This is where 90% of small businesses fail.

But persevere beyond this point. Just like a marriage, learn to scale back your pride and make compromises. Learn to communicate with everyone: suppliers, customers, staff, your bank manager, your family who don’t see much of you any more because you’re working all hours. Make sacrifices for the greater good. Do you really need to be in that trendy office or can you work from a garage or coffee shop for a while? Do you really need to spend money on that slick stationery? Do you really need to work with that horrible client who causes you so much stress and doesn’t even pay you? Do you really need to keep that member of staff or can you do their work yourself (firing someone is one of the hardest things you will ever do, by the way).

Persevere and you will start to understand what’s really important (hint: it’s cashflow; if you focus on only one thing, let it be that) and gain the confidence to make decisions that will uphold the important things. A healthy business will start to emerge with a strong, battle-worn owner at the helm. There will be more struggles on the road ahead but each battle won adds a layer of self-satisfaction and confidence that simply does not compare to any employed position. It will transform you as a person and set you up for life, because you will never be at the mercy of the job market again.

The hardest part of owning and running a business is sustaining disciplined, reality‑based decision-making while balancing competing time, cash, and people pressures. That single line unpacks into four tightly linked, enduring challenges:

1. Managing cash-flow and financial fragility

- Cash, not profit, kills businesses. Forecasting cash flow, timing receivables/payables, and maintaining an operational runway are constant tasks.
- Unexpected expenses, seasonal variations, and client-payment delays require buffers (line of credit, reserve) and conservative projections.
- Pricing decisions must reflect cost, market, and long-term viability; cutting price to win business erodes sustainability.

1. Hiring, developing and keeping the right people

- Recruiting candidates who fit role, culture and future needs is time‑consuming; hires often fail because of unclear role design or rushed processes.
- Retention requires clear career paths, meaningful feedback, fair compensation and aligned incentives. Founder/manager attention to culture and onboarding is critical.
- Delegation and building reliable management beneath the owner are hard but essential to scale.

1. Prioritization under limited time and attention

- Owners face constant urgent demands (clients, payroll, operations) that crowd out strategic work (product, growth, systems).
- Establishing routines and decision frameworks — time-blocking for high‑value activities, KPIs that trigger action, and rules for escalation — prevents reactivity.
- Saying “no” to good opportunities is often harder than it sounds but necessary to avoid dilution of focus.

1. Adapting strategy to market realities while avoiding short‑termism

- Founders must test assumptions cheaply, interpret data correctly, and pivot when warranted without chasing every shiny idea.
- Balancing investment in product and customer acquisition versus immediate revenue needs is a continual tension.
- Maintaining customer-centric discipline (measure retention, unit economics) disciplines growth.

Secondary but pervasive difficulties

- Regulatory, legal and compliance burden that grows with scale and geography.
- Emotional load: isolation, anxiety about payroll and reputation, and the pressure of personal financial exposure.
- Operational complexity: systems, processes, supply-chain reliability and vendor management become significant time sinks.

Practical patterns that separate surviving owners from thriving ones

- Ruthless discipline on cash and unit economics before aggressive scaling.
- Hire slowly; document roles and develop one reliable manager before hiring a dozen.
- Build a predictable cadence: weekly metrics review, monthly financial close, quarterly strategy checkpoints.
- Automate and standardize repetitive processes early to free owner bandwidth.
- Create a decision framework (e.g., thresholds for hiring, customer acquisition cost limits, runway minimums) so emotions don’t drive critical choices.

Examples (typical stories)

- A fast-growing startup that scaled sales but neglected gross margins and ran out of cash despite rising revenue — solved by pausing hiring, renegotiating vendor terms, and refocusing on higher‑margin segments.

Two Things, among many.

1. Money.
2. Self Belief.

Money:

You need to earn a lot more money to justify that you would do OK. I would strongly suggest you read a book called Rich Dad Poor Dad and try a combination of job with your own work / business unless it gives you enough money that you can easily leave your full time job.

Self Belief:

You never let go of your self-belief in you. Even at times when things are not going your way, you need to hold your ground. Become more flexible with your approach, business model and everything else to ensure that you are able to make your business work.

I would strongly recommend start low with your own business and gradually grow it if you do not have savings to sustain you without a full time job.

But if you do not have any option and must have to work full time to make your venture work out, save up some money to go by for at least several months.

- And once you have that much money, work your \*\*\*\* off to make your business work.
- Work extra hours if you have to.
- Focus on the cash inflows.
- Make your customers happy and keep them happy.
- Learn more quickly and make adjustments on the go instead of sinking all your money.
- If it involves investing money, start low and gradually increase the investment.

Hope this helps answer your question.

Lack of social interaction. I was a computer programmer. Not all programmers are antisocial nerds. My team was mostly extroverts. Lots of daily interaction. Lots of collaboration. In the case of junior programmers, I spent hours telling stories of problems my teams had tackled over the years and what techniques we used to find those solutions. That is a primary way people learn to solve problems. We also held regular brainstorming sessions that improved productivity for all the members.

Then came the pandemic. After a few weeks in isolation I was lonesome for my team. Some members, including me, were really suffering. Zoom meetings were full of somber faces. Productivity suffered. I anticipated people dusting off their resumes. As winter blossomed into spring I invited everyone on the team to join me for lunch; in an open parking lot each member bringing a lunch and a lawn chair; with proper social distancing. We had great fun. We started each lunch just talking and eating, like we would have done if we had walked out of our cubicles and met in tbe break room. After a half hour or so, the conversations turned to work. We shared our various difficulties. We brainstormed. The juices started flowing. We were re-invigorated. We usually spent one and a half or two hours at lunch but very little time was spent eating. Then we all went back home and implemented what we had worked out. Not all team members came. Not everyone came every week but all were welcome. If you needed a friend fix you came. If you didn’t you didn’t. I went every week. I draw energy from working in a group.

Dealing with people.

People are ALWAYS the hardest part of any business.

Real estate is plagued with enormously high costs, due to its nature as a necessity. Without suitable shelter, people will die, except in the most temperate of regions. This makes it easy to charge insane amounts of money for doing ANYTHING even remotely related to the industry, because unlike food, (which is also a necessity), it is rather difficult to produce even minimally adequate shelter. It always astonishes me, how long it takes someone to build, or to even just remodel, their house.

That means that EVERYBODY is competing for “a place to live”, as people don’t want to live near other crazy people. However, as it turns out, EVERYBODY is crazy, so they make compromises. Apartments buildings are one such compromise.

I am learning about the ins and outs of running a small apartment building. I am not very good at it at the moment. When I started working on the project, there were 33% vacant units, with various amounts of filth and damage needing to be repaired. SO much damage, that they cannot legally be rented out to anyone yet. It took me over an hour and a half to clean JUST the tub/shower in the upstairs bathroom of the unit that I moved into. I wouldn’t even get into it, before I cleaned it, for the first two weeks. I am not even talking about the stove, refrigerator, wait, THAT was missing, dirtiest carpets in the world, (even up the stairs), missing lightbulbs, and nasty brown ring around the toilet, nor the holes in all of the cheap, hollow core doors, nor the paint overspray on the windows from the idiots who couldn’t even be bothered to tape off the windows before painting the exterior, nor bird pooh from them perching on the warm street light on the corner of the building. Keep in mind, that I moved into the most NEARLY habitable unit possible! I have never needed more than 5 minutes to clean ANY bathtub that I have ever wanted to use in my entire life! I have been here two months now, and NOT ONE of the vacant units is ready yet. I DID, however, manage to get the city to remove the red, “condemned” notice from one unit, by finishing up the repairs sufficiently that had been started THREE YEARS prior to my arriving.

Am I in over my head? Probably.

People who are treated badly act like pigs. Pigs leave messes behind. For revenge.

People who worked in family businesses and weren't family, what were the things you disliked most about it?

I haven’t worked in a family business but in my former career in finance we frequently used to buy family businesses and we’d very often continue to employ executive level non-family members, some of whom I became business friendly with.

The most frequent complains that I heard were:

- There was often no ability to advance past a certain point. A designated family member was going to become the CEO, or the VP of Marketing, or some position that a non-family member would likely have become had it been a normal business. Maybe that person didn’t have their eyes set on the CEO chair but they did want to head up marketing and that was never going to happen.
- Depending on the size of the company and the family there could be what I’d call the “stupid nephew” situation. It wasn’t necessarily a nephew, it could be a son/daughter, a cousin or whomever, but that person was going to hold a lower or mid level position that they were not qualified for. Maybe they weren’t low on the IQ scale and they were just lazy because they were from a wealthy family and never had to put forth effort, but that person is bullet proof. They also are typically paid far higher than the market for that position and that sits on your P&L. They can not be fired and they can do no wrong, so you have dead weight in your organization and can’t do anything about it. We sold one of our companies to a decent sized publicly traded company that was controlled by a family and one of the cousins was the “stupid nephew.” His title was something like VP of Special Projects. I’m pretty sure the job description was to golf four days per week and get a nice afternoon buzz going as he came back to the office. His salary was $300,000 per year, but at least he wasn’t keeping a non family member from advancing.
- Decisions can be made that make no business sense and are all about family dynamics. Depending on how many generations are involved there can be a lot of stupid great aunts and uncles, aunts and uncles, cousins and second cousins and so on, and they may all have a say in major decisions. Those decisions may not be best for the business but they’re settling old childhood scores. My mom worked for a family owned real estate investment and development firm that was going onto the fourth generation. Only one part of the family had been active in managing the business going on two generations and they were really good, but ownership was split up amongst multiple distant family members who all sucked from the family business’ teats. My family is very weird and they don’t make any sense.

Last weekend my grandma told me to get ready fast. We’re going somewhere. I asked her where and she said there’s no time. Get dressed. I’ll be in the SUV. I run around like a loonatic putting a dress on and my phone rings. It’s my grandma telling me to hurry up. I run out of the house and hop in the back of the SUV. Then she tells me I’m too dressed up to go to Publix, and then says it’s fine we have to go and tells Dave to drive because we’re in a hurry. I asked her why we’re in a hurry. She said she wants to get there before their Mojito rotisserie chicken is gone. Like wtf…she got me all freaked out over a freaking chicken. I crawled all the way to the back, and screamed into the seat next to me stomping my feet. Then she tells me to sit down and stop acting like a child.

Then yesterday my grandma decided we’re eating out. She tells me we should go to the sushi place because I like sushi, so I got all psyched. She called my parents and told them to meet us at the restaurant. When we got there and sat down at the table, she tells me don’t order raw fish. I’m like..wtf..that’s what sushi is. Anyways I ordered sushi. Then my parents showed up late. My order arrived and my dad made a comment about my order and so did my grandma. I just ignored them.

I woke up at 1 am because I got thirsty. There’s a bunch of freaking people in the house. My parents asked me why I’m walking around in my nighty with all these guests around. Wtf..I’m trying to get to the f’ing kitchen to get f’ing water and nobody told me about a f’ing party. I can’t get back to my bedroom because my dad doesn’t want me walking around not dressed appropriately so I’m laying on the sofa in the study….it’s 3:20 am and I can’t sleep because it’s too f’ing noisy.

These are the screwed up things I have to deal with in my family. Then I also have to deal with high school crap.

It depends.

For someone who owns less than $250,000 in investable assets, that’s a typical fee. Most advisors require a lower fee the more money they are managing for you, and vice versa.

A better question to ask is, “Is that fee fair for the value I will receive?”

I pay others to change the oil in my car, manage my landscaping, press my clothes, or prepare a meal for me. Why would I pay someone else when I’m perfectly capable of doing all these things for myself? Because they save me time.

The simple truth is there are a lot of people who simply don’t want to do it themselves and are willing to pay someone else to outsource that aspect of their life so they can stay focused on other things. Of course, they expect a great deal of value in exchange.

Here is a sampling of the other work I do for my clients that is included in the annual fee they pay:

- Creating, amending, and monitoring their retirement income plan.
- Periodically rebalancing their portfolio, including accounts I don’t manage and don’t get paid for, like a 401(k)
- Managing cash flow
- Managing taxable income
- Researching Medicare and long term care insurance plans
- Identifying and vetting CPAs, trust attorneys, real estate professionals, and anyone else they need
- Providing education so they know the right questions to ask
- Providing guidance to their children and grandchildren at no additional cost

A good advisor will make your life easier and add value. An advisor who only recommends investments or insurance is likely a salesperson.

Paying a financial advisor 2% of your total portfolio is generally considered on the high side for advisory fees. Here are some points to consider:

1. Industry Standards: Typical fees for financial advisors range from 0.5% to 1% of assets under management (AUM). A 2% fee may be justified if the advisor provides exceptional services, such as comprehensive financial planning, tax optimization, or estate planning.
2. Value of Services: Evaluate what services the advisor is providing. If they are offering personalized investment strategies, regular financial reviews, or access to exclusive investment opportunities, a higher fee might be more acceptable.
3. Performance: Consider the advisor's track record. If they consistently outperform the market or add significant value through their advice, a higher fee might be worthwhile.
4. Alternatives: Explore other options, such as robo-advisors, which often charge lower fees (around 0.25% to 0.5%) for basic portfolio management, or consider negotiating fees with your current advisor.
5. Personal Financial Situation: Assess how the fee impacts your overall investment returns and whether it aligns with your financial goals.

In summary, while 2% is higher than average, the appropriateness of this fee depends on the value you receive in return. Consider your specific needs and the advisor's qualifications before making a decision.

Financial Advisors can change lives, but I often see some big mistakes that can drastically affect the results - even as bad as losing all investment gains made! With all the new-age scams out there, it really matters to know these mistakes - so pay attention!

Here’s the Most Common Mistakes:

Hiring a “Financial Advisor” who is not a Fiduciary

Anybody can be a “financial advisor”. It’s an unregulated term so the title means nothing. Not going to name names, but plenty of companies out there abuse this term. Just watch “The Wolf Of Wall Street” to see more on how that works!

On the other hand, there’s a key type of financial advisor called a “Fidicuary” and they are what you want. They are legally obligated to make financial decisions that are in your best interest. They are the good guys on your side, and they can even lose their license if they do anything deemed unethical by regulators.

You can find fiduciaries near you on websites like [ComparisonAdviser.com](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Advisors%20Keywords%20-%20Questions&utm_content=What%20are%20some%20mistakes%20that%20people%20can%20make%20when%20choosing%20a%20financial%20advisor%3F&qclid=448700220346132110%7C211106241908221%7C0 "trk.heysaver.com") \- their network is exclusively vetted and regulated fiduciaries.

Hiring the first advisor you meet

Ideally you are looking for a long-term partner in this space. It’s best to interview more than one advisor before making your final decision. Do what feels right for you.

Not checking their credentials

This goes without saying…make sure the advisor has the credentials and licenses to handle your finances. It never hurts to ask about tests they have taken as well! As I mentioned above, the highest-standard are called [fiduciaries](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4

“Sell my investments. I want out. I’ll get back in when everything looks ok,” a client tells me on the phone.

“Tell me what the day looks like when everything is ok and you have a plan. Otherwise you only have a fantasy,” I respond.

I can’t tell you how many times I’ve heard that. And if I wasn’t there to talk them down they’d have jumped.

It’s why studies show the average self directed fund investor makes 1/3 the average return of the stock market.

Not because they don’t have the investments available. They do.

But they buy high when everyone is euphoric and sell low when everyone is panicked.

Or chase the latest hot dot fad, jumping in just as it crests and collapses.

Or a hundred other creative ways people invent to blow themselves up financially.

So no you don’t need an adviser to buy an investment. Those days ended a long time ago.

But you may need an adviser to protect you from yourself.

And that’s the snake you don’t see that may bite you.

Financial Advisors can change lives, but I often see some big mistakes that can drastically affect the results - even as bad as losing all investment gains made! With all the new-age scams out there, it really matters to know these mistakes - so pay attention!

Here’s the Most Common Mistakes:

Hiring a “Financial Advisor” who is not a Fiduciary

Anybody can be a “financial advisor”. It’s an unregulated term so the title means nothing. Not going to name names, but plenty of companies out there abuse this term. Just watch “The Wolf Of Wall Street” to see more on how that works!

On the other hand, there’s a key type of financial advisor called a “Fidicuary” and they are what you want. They are legally obligated to make financial decisions that are in your best interest. They are the good guys on your side, and they can even lose their license if they do anything deemed unethical by regulators.

You can find fiduciaries near you on websites like [ComparisonAdviser.com](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Advisors%20Keywords%20-%20Questions&utm_content=What%20are%20some%20mistakes%20that%20people%20can%20make%20when%20choosing%20a%20financial%20advisor%3F&qclid=2090811339462171200%7C211106241908221%7C0 "trk.heysaver.com") \- their network is exclusively vetted and regulated fiduciaries.

Hiring the first advisor you meet

Ideally you are looking for a long-term partner in this space. It’s best to interview more than one advisor before making your final decision. Do what feels right for you.

Not checking their credentials

This goes without saying…make sure the advisor has the credentials and licenses to handle your finances. It never hurts to ask about tests they have taken as well! As I mentioned above, the highest-standard are called [fiduciaries](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Advisors%20Keywords%20-%20Questions&utm_content=What%20are%20some%20mistakes%20that%20people%20can%20make%20when%20choosing%20a%20financial%20advisor%3F&qclid=2090811339462171200%7C211106241908221%7C0 "trk.heysaver.com") so you can expect them to have all their ducks in a row.

Not hiring a specialist that meets your specific needs
