People new to investing intuitively think that a commission-based advisor offers “free” advisory services since they are not directly paying them. Rather, the purchased commission-based investment balance silently drops as the advisor peels off their commissions.

Couple things I’ve observed over the years:

1. Commissions can be in the form of a “front load” where it’s largely paid upfront or it can be one that provides the advisor a regular (usually yearly) “trail commission”. Either way, your investment return is eroded
2. Commission-based products can include high “expense ratio” investments. For example, a normal stock or bond fund whose yearly expense ratio is less than 1% could cost you 1.5% or more. Watch those fees since it automatically (and silently) cuts into your yearly return
3. Many commission-based Advisors have weak or non-certified qualifications. They are NOT “Fiduciaries” meaning will sell you “suitable” investment but they are NOT obligated to sell you the “best” as they choose a product that pays them quietly, a hefty commission. They therefore have a “conflict of interest”. Monikers such as CFS (certified funds specialist) only means they passed a series of Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) license exams.

On the other end of the spectrum are Registered Investment Advisors (RIA). They are required by oath and law to researching and selling you the “BEST” products for your personal situation.

1. RIAs such as Certified Financial Planners (CFP) and Chartered Financial Analysts (CFA) have undergone rigorous multi-year training/education and passed exams before they can become your “Fiduciary”.
2. You pay them a “fee” for their services. The most common fee is a “Fee-only” yearly fee type (usually as a percentage of total assets under management: “AUM”). Others charge a “flat fee” or if you are lucky, an hourly rate amount (much like a CPA). Others are “fee-based” which can include some of the above plus commissions if you are regularly buying/selling assets.

In summary, RIAs (being more thoroughly educated) will typically do much legwork to research, compare and recommend low expense ratio investment products tailored for your personal situation whereas commission-based advisors are more like salesmen, selecting vehicles that are “OK” but providing them a fat commission. Simply stated, buyer beware.

As with most questions along this line, there is no single correct answer - much depends on what your goals are for the next 5 years or so. But roughly:

1/ Pay off any debt.

I assume you've already done this. Only real exception I'd make is mortgage debt assuming it's < 4% APR and you're getting the mortgage interest deduction on taxes.

2/ Are you planning on buying a house within the next 5 years?

If so, figure out your budget for that and set it aside in fairly safe investments (e.g. TIPS, treasuries, munis, maybe some high-grade corporates). Equities have historically given better returns over the long term, but that certainly isn't guaranteed over a 5 year time frame.

3/ Are you planning on living off this money, or saving it until retirement?

The sooner you'll need to draw down this amount, the less risky you want to be. $5M is good, but not really enough to live on for decades of time if you want to preserve purchasing power and live an upper-middle class lifestyle.

A lot of people that build up some savings opt to take 6 months or a year off, but eventually re-join the workforce.

4/ Are you planning on doing angel investing?

You have enough money, especially if you're not living off of it, where you could reasonably start doing some angel investing. If this is so, you're probably doing it to stay involved with companies, people, the industry, etc... over pure financial return. Set aside $1M or so, which gives you 20 bullets at $25k each.

5/ So you've decided on a house (or to not get one), you've thought about angel investing, you have an idea of short term income needs you'll want from the $$. So the real question: do it yourself, use an advisor, or something in the middle.

Advisors are useful, but not because they're financial geniuses, rather they have access to investments you couldn't get otherwise. Unfortunately many of those investments are fairly illiquid and may have $1M+ minimums. This means they may not be ideal for someone with a $5M portfolio (closer to $10M and it starts making more sense). Imagine taking 20% of your money on a speculative investment and not being able to touch it for 24 months.

So instead, I'd probably try to emphasize diversity across asset classes and low fees. Fortunately, there's a service that does exactly this already: Wealthfront. They work to build a diversified portfolio across many low-cost ETFs, while only taking 25 basis points from you (25% the cost of many financial advisors). I'd check them out, and then re-consider the financial advisor once you've doubled up to $10M.

Not even close, most hedge fund managers actually struggle, particularly in the first few years. This is in large part due to the absolutely insane amounts of rules and regulations a small firm has to comply with. I’ll rant a little about how this came about, then provide some figures for you.

It is no big secret that big firms on Wall Street (primarily JP Morgan and Goldman) had a big hand in drafting Dodd Frank and updating the SEC (and various state) regulations. There were comments made that this was specifically done to eliminate small hedge funds that were providing the bigger firms with quite a bit of competition. It’s government collusion at its best and a great example of corporations using the government to hurt others. Multi-billion dollar firms can stomach an increase in compliance cost - small businesses cannot.

And let’s not pretend that the regulatory changes protected customers any more than the TSA changing the regulations on liquid bottles from 4 oz to 3.6 oz protects passengers. There were already rules and laws on the books to prevent both the Madoff issue and the mortgage collapse (which was almost 100% caused by a combination of government laws, the FED, and agreements with the large banks…but that’s another topic).

I run a small firm. I get audited regularly because I do businesses in a number of states. An audit normally takes multiple days (once it took 12 days) and multiple auditors (not infrequent to have 3–6 government employees in the office). They check things like making sure my files are in alphabetical order, that my security license is hung the right number of inches off the floor, that my bills going to my clients are written in an approved font, that my signature block on my email messages contained the government approved statements, and the like. I’ve been chastised for having sent bills on letters with too small of margins.

Do you know how many times a government auditor has taken my bank account statements, my brokerage statements, my client statements, and reconciled them to make sure everything was calculated? Once. It happened for the first time this year. When I’ve asked why they don’t check things to ensure that firms aren’t actually stealing client money I get told the auditors really don’t have the education to do that (which is why we also have a requirement to have an independent auditor review everything).

The number of rules and regulations I have to comply with is well North of 100,000 pages.

There are many facets to this position. To a certain degree, this is a sales position. You have to be able to go out there, develop relationships, and establish trust so people will entrust their life savings to you. I have been in it for close to two decades now and it has had its up and downs. When the market is up, clients think you have something to do with it and when the market goes down, they tend to blame you.

I would say the most important aspect is to keep the clients interest always first. That will help. There is the temptation to chase after the latest investment, but from my experience (and learning from my mistakes), often the key isn’t the investment, but making sure you create a good financial plan and making sure that the right investments fit the plan.

Like many other industries, once you gain a solid reputation, it will be easier to gain clients. If you are able to join a large group and inherit or share the client asset base, then that will make it significantly easier. I would say this job is all about relationships.

If you like the number crunching aspect of investing and not dealing with clients, then you can look at being an analyst, trader, or essentially a pure investment manager.

It's definitely not hard with your credentials. All you need is your insurance licenses (life, accident & health), your Series 7 and Series 66 licenses. My goal is to get a CFP after a couple years of experience but that's down the road. All I have is a bachelor's in accounting and I got a position as an advisor a couple months after graduation, when I decided to ditch my other job as a financial consultant in the derivatives remediation market. Even some of the people that came into the company after me aren't from finance backgrounds (one has a bachelor's in psychology).

But definitely look more into what being a financial advisor actually entails. I learned the actual title of being a "financial advisor" is pretty much a marketing tool, to make people feel they are talking to someone who is doing everything in their best interests (I'm not saying advisors don't do things in the best interest, but that advisors also consider their pay when they offer their advice, so that there is a mutual benefit in their services that they provide). There is a high turnover in this industry and I'm coming to understand it from first hand experience. There is a lot of marketing (in my case, cold calling) in the beginning as you build your book of business from scratch. And working on commission can be a little nerve racking, not having a steady source of income (unless you work for a company that offers a salary, which I don't).

Then you realize, this isn't really an advisory position, but somewhat of a sales position, where you have a bit of a bias "advising people" to go with certain products. If you work for a big insurance company, you're probably going to focus on selling variable universal life insurance products since they offer the highest commissions, and if for a wire house company, you'll be pushing brokerage accounts, (but if you work for a company like Fidelity you'll probably have to meet challenging quotas (I think 10 million your first year, don't quote me on that)). Don't get me wrong, the products still have to pass a suitability test, but it doesn't feel as fulfilling as you thought it would feel like when you were expecting to be in it 110% for your clients (but I guess you can call me naive for thinking that).

I'm also a person who likes perfecting their craft, in this case, my knowledge on finance (markets, analysis, economic events, etc). The only education I'm getting is the Series 7, Series 66, and life insurance exam study materials, plus the occasional informational meetings we have a couple days every week. But that's it. I learn everything I want to further understand on my own. I recently got myself a tax book I'm reading so I can provide better advice to my clients, and eventually become registered as a tax preparer so that I can expand the scope of my business. I want to get my CFA, but that will also be on my own time and under my own expense.

But I did I get a lot out of this position thusfar, and a lot of these skills are transferable not only to other jobs, but to your life as well.

In the United States, there are so many different ways to be a financial advisor. Finding work-life balance is a matter of finding the right way for you.

Being a financial advisor comes with a great deal of responsibility. You are advising people about their money, risks to their money, and most importantly how to use their money to support their desired lifestyle. Having work-life balance in such a role requires mentally turning off work from time to time, even though it may seem that your help is needed at all times. By definition, you have to balance the needs of clients, of family, and of an employer (even if that’s yourself).

It took a few hard lessons for me to learn work-life balance as a financial advisor. I don’t think these lessons are unique to this career. You could probably replace “financial advisor” with “lawyer” or “resident physician” or “accountant,” and find similar stories from other professions.

1. Early in my career, working for a bank, I worked with ultra high net worth individuals and families. These were people who were accustomed to having others drop whatever they were doing to cater to their requests. Some took this seriously and were a pleasure to work with while others abused it. Being prone to people-pleasing, I had no boundaries as to when clients could call me. I felt as though I “owed” it to them to work on whatever they were asking. One year I remember it was my birthday. I had officially taken the day off from work, but a client whose name was on a downtown skyscraper called my cell. I answered as I was leaving the grocery store. It turned out to be his second in command, who berated me, yelling, about how long it was taking to get a difficult, complex transaction done. I listened, apologized, and told him I would handle it. When we hung up, my stomach was in knots. I didn’t know at the time I didn’t have to take that call, I didn’t have to do what he asked, and I certainly didn’t have to take his abuse. The bank didn’t tell me I had to - I just thought I did - but they didn’t tell me I didn’t have to, either.
2. In the middle of my career, I was recruited to join the trust arm of a well-known brokerage firm. It was a new start-up division focused on providing services as a fiduciary. I loved that idea. The boss promised it would be exciting and fun to help craft the division from scratch. What he didn’t say was that he would be spending corporate money to sponsor fundraising events almost seven days a week, and that I would be expected to attend to court potential new clients. I didn’t happen to agree with this strategy. As far as I could tell, all it produced was the boss gaining power by then being asked to serve on local charity boards because of his “largesse.” Nevertheless before I knew it I was working into nearly every evening entertaining people at fundraising tables, charity events and sports venues. It sounds glamorous, but it got old quickly. I was tired all the time. I realized I had made a mistake and left after 16 months, in 2006.

By the gist of your question, you are referring to close to death. A financial planner will have to refer you to an estate planner for various documents. Assuming that there are substantial assets, that would entail a revocable trust (which become irrevocable at death) detailing who gets what and when. If you have a special needs beneficiary, that is another trust. You will need a will for miscellaneous items not covered in the trust. You will need powers of attorneys which give rights to another to ‘speak’ for you if you become incapacitated. There is another document for health care regarding how you want to die (basically extensive procedures to prolong life no matter what or perhaps DNR- do not Resuscitate ).

Probably best to do this first since a planner will simply tell you you need a trust and…..…….

Once this is completed and there still financial issues at hand, just pay a planner for that work.

If that was to occur now, recognize that the inverted yield curve has indicated a recession- and it has been 100% correct over the past 50 years. There were losses of 49% in 2000 and 57% in 2008.

You do NOT want to get hammered by losing 50% in the next recession. It would be prudent to revise your portfolio.

Having been a financial Advisor with a reputable company, (key thing), I can tell you not to worry too much about education, or licensing, as that should be an even playing field. What you want is:

1. EXPERIENCE. How long have they been doing this job? How did they handle the financial crisis in 2008? Or even before that?
2. Will they listen to YOU? Before I was a financial advisor, MY financial advisors would call me and want me to invest on the spot on their word alone. Although I TRUSTED them or I wouldn’t have worked with them, I ALWAYS did my own research too (even before being fully trained to do so). I am a conservative investor. I am NOT going to jump in an instant just because you called.
3. DO YOU TRUST THEM? You may have to meet with several financial advisors before you find a person you feel you basically can trust. This is not easy to get at with questions. It’s more a sense you get after spending time with a person.
4. For their average portfolio, what kind of returns do they see? Further delineate by conservative portfolios and aggressive portfolios, depending on how you see yourself.
5. Give them a quick scenario of your situation, and ask for some QUICK suggestions. Be fair about this. REAL suggestions take time and a full analysis of your current situation. But watch if they had listened to your goals, and your risk tolerance.

Good luck

In the accountancy world “financial planning” is normally called “budgeting”, just one of many activities undertaken by accountants.

Financial Advisors can change lives, but I often see some big mistakes that can drastically affect the results - even as bad as losing all investment gains made! With all the new-age scams out there, it really matters to know these mistakes - so pay attention!

Here’s the Most Common Mistakes:

Hiring a “Financial Advisor” who is not a Fiduciary

Anybody can be a “financial advisor”. It’s an unregulated term so the title means nothing. Not going to name names, but plenty of companies out there abuse this term. Just watch “The Wolf Of Wall Street” to see more on how that works!

On the other hand, there’s a key type of financial advisor called a “Fidicuary” and they are what you want. They are legally obligated to make financial decisions that are in your best interest. They are the good guys on your side, and they can even lose their license if they do anything deemed unethical by regulators.

You can find fiduciaries near you on websites like [ComparisonAdviser.com](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Advisors%20Keywords%20-%20Questions&utm_content=What%20are%20some%20mistakes%20that%20people%20can%20make%20when%20choosing%20a%20financial%20advisor%3F&qclid=519757155643941637%7C211106241908221%7C0 "trk.heysaver.com") \- their network is exclusively vetted and regulated fiduciaries.

Hiring the first advisor you meet

Ideally you are looking for a long-term partner in this space. It’s best to interview more than one advisor before making your final decision. Do what feels right for you.

Not checking their credentials

This goes without saying…make sure the advisor has the credentials and licenses to handle your finances. It never hurts to ask about tests they have taken as well! As I mentioned above, the highest-standard are called [fiduciaries](https://trk.heysaver.com/c6de229a-39db-4f4f-8683-d74fa6c856e2?utm_campaign=FA%20KW&utm_medium=Financial%20Advisors%20Keywords%20-%20Q

Really interesting question.

I am not sure if there are more detailed studies out there, but one of my business mentors once told me that when he was involved in hiring and training of new advisors for a large financial services firm, their numbers were 6 out of 12 new hires would still be with the company 1 year later, 3 of those would still be there 3 years later and only 1 of the 12 would still be with the firm 5 years later.

They planned for an attrition rate of about 92%.

Now some of these would have moved on to another firm and some of those would have become “successful” somewhere else, but I would say from my personal experience that the number is somewhere between 10–20% of people who get licensed and try to become an advisor are successful at it.

Many, like me, go on to be successful at something else :)
